notes: Hand-rewritten exemplar set (manual; for the drafter to learn from). Same cited sources as the draft. Per-review calibration applied. For the $800 tier, 'go a level deeper': clue a lesser-known entity or a specialist sub-fact (Epicurus's ataraxia, Medusa as the only mortal Gorgon) rather than an oblique angle on a household name — oblique-on-marquee tops out ~$600. Grounded only in the cited Wikipedia docs.

questions.clue: For slipping mortals a gift stolen from the gods of Olympus, this defiant Titan was bound to a crag where an eagle returned each day to feast on his ever-regrowing liver.

citations.title: Prometheus
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Prometheus is a Titan responsible for creating or aiding humanity in its earliest days. He defied the Olympian gods by taking fire from them and giving it to humanity in the form of technology, knowledge and, more genera

questions.clue: God of healing yet also of plague, of music and of the far-shooting bow, this deity was held up by the Greeks as the very ideal of youthful male beauty — the kouros made divine.

citations.title: Apollo
citations.snippet: Apollo is one of the Olympian deities in ancient Greek religion and mythology. His numerous functions include healing, prophecy, music, poetry, and archery. He is the son of Zeus and Leto, and the twin brother of Artemis, goddess of the hun

questions.clue: After the three brothers who toppled the Titans split the cosmos by lot, this eldest of them drew neither the sky nor the sea, but the realm of the dead.

citations.title: Hades
citations.snippet: Hades, in the ancient Greek religion and mythology, is the god of the dead and riches and the King of the underworld, with which his name became synonymous. Hades was the eldest son of Cronus and Rhea, although this also made him the last s

questions.clue: Bronze Age clay tablets at Pylos rank this 'Earth-Shaker' among the very chief gods — venerated there centuries before the classical age recast him mainly as lord of the sea.

citations.title: Poseidon
citations.snippet: Poseidon is one of the twelve Olympians in ancient Greek religion and mythology, presiding over the sea, storms, earthquakes and horses. He was the protector of seafarers and the guardian of many Hellenic cities and colonies. In pre-Olympia

questions.clue: Swinging the hammer Mjölnir against the giants of chaos, this red-bearded god was the steadfast protector of mankind and of the Viking farmer.

citations.title: Thor
citations.snippet: Thor is a prominent god in Germanic paganism. In Norse mythology, he is a hammer-wielding god associated with thunder, storms, strength, protection, fertility, and farmers. Besides Old Norse Þórr, the deity occurs in Old English as Thunor,

questions.clue: By a giantess this trickster sired three bringers of doom — the wolf Fenrir, the world-serpent, and the death-queen Hel.

citations.title: Loki
citations.snippet: Loki is a god in Norse mythology. He is the son of Fárbauti and Laufey, and the brother of Helblindi and Býleistr. Loki is married to the goddess Sigyn and they have two sons, Narfi or Nari and Váli. By the jötunn Angrboða, Loki is the fath

questions.clue: Each dawn he loosed two ravens — Thought and Memory — to range across the nine worlds and murmur back at dusk all they had witnessed.

citations.title: Odin
citations.snippet: Odin is a widely revered god in Norse mythology and Germanic paganism. Most surviving information on Odin comes from Norse mythology, but he figures prominently in the recorded history of Northern Europe. This includes the Roman Empire's pa

questions.clue: Egyptians held that this goddess's magic ushered souls safely into the afterlife, and that every reigning pharaoh was the living Horus she had borne and shielded.

citations.title: Isis
citations.snippet: Isis was a major goddess in ancient Egyptian religion whose worship spread throughout the Greco-Roman world. Isis was first mentioned in the Old Kingdom as one of the main characters of the Osiris myth, in which she resurrects her slain bro

questions.clue: Protector of graves and lord of embalming, this canine-headed Egyptian god led the newly dead along the road to the underworld and its judgment.

citations.title: Anubis
citations.snippet: Anubis, also known as Inpu, Inpw, Jnpw, or Anpu in Ancient Egyptian, is the god of funerary rites, protector of graves, and guide to the underworld in ancient Egyptian religion, usually depicted as a canine or a man with a canine head.

questions.clue: Shown as a mummy in a tall plumed crown, gripping a crook and flail, this lord of grain and the dead let Egyptians read the harvest's yearly withering and regrowth as his own.

citations.title: Osiris
citations.snippet: Osiris was the god of fertility, agriculture, the afterlife, the dead, resurrection, life, and vegetation in ancient Egyptian religion. He was classically depicted with a pharaoh's beard, partially mummy-wrapped at the legs, wearing a disti

questions.clue: Professing that he knew only that he knew nothing, this Athenian set down not a single word of his own — yet his needling question-and-answer cross-examination survives through the pens of his students.

citations.title: Socrates
citations.snippet: Socrates was an ancient Greek philosopher from Classical Athens, perhaps the first Western moral philosopher, and a major inspiration on his student Plato, who largely founded the tradition of Western philosophy. An enigmatic figure, Socrat

questions.clue: This Enlightenment philosopher staged a 'Copernican' turn in thought: space, time, and cause are not features of the world but forms the mind imposes — so the thing-in-itself stays forever beyond knowing.

citations.title: Immanuel Kant
citations.snippet: Immanuel Kant was a German philosopher. Born in Königsberg in the Kingdom of Prussia, he is considered one of the central thinkers of the Enlightenment. His comprehensive and systematic works in epistemology, metaphysics, logic, ethics, aes

questions.clue: A classical-philology prodigy made a Basel professor at just 24, this German later proclaimed that 'God is dead' and conjured the Übermensch and eternal recurrence before madness took him at 44.

citations.title: Friedrich Nietzsche
citations.snippet: Friedrich Wilhelm Nietzsche was a German philosopher and writer who started his career as a classical philologist and turned to philosophy early in his academic career. In 1869, at age 24, he was appointed Professor of Classical Philology a

questions.clue: This ancient philosophical tradition, founded in the Hellenistic period, held that virtue alone is sufficient for happiness and that the universe operates according to reason, called logos.

citations.title: Stoicism
citations.snippet: Stoicism is a philosophical movement and practical guide to living, emphasizing daily self-discipline and moral improvement, which originated in the Hellenistic period of ancient Greece and proliferated well into the Roman Imperial period.

questions.clue: Arising some 2,500 years ago as a śramaṇa movement on the eastern Gangetic plain, this faith draws its name and its path from a teacher remembered as 'the Awakened One.'

citations.title: Buddhism
citations.snippet: Buddhism, also known as Buddhadharma and Dharmavinaya, is an Indian religion and philosophy based on teachings attributed to the Buddha, a śramaṇa and religious teacher who lived in the 6th or 5th century BCE. It is the world's fourth-large

questions.clue: This Hellenistic thinker taught that the highest good is a calm freedom from bodily pain and mental disturbance — won not by indulgence but by mastering one's desires, dispelling needless fears, and prizing friendship.

citations.title: Epicurus
citations.snippet: Epicurus was an ancient Greek philosopher who founded the philosophical school of Epicureanism, which taught that management of one's desires, removal of unnecessary fears, friendship, and virtuous living led to a pleasant life and constant

questions.clue: Alone among her three monstrous sisters in being mortal, this daughter of the primordial sea-deities Phorcys and Ceto turned to stone anyone who met her gaze.

citations.title: Medusa
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Medusa, also called Gorgo or the Gorgon, was one of the three Gorgons. Medusa is generally described as a woman with living snakes in place of hair; her appearance was so hideous that anyone who looked upon her was turne

questions.clue: This Titaness — her name the Greek root of our very word for memory — lay with Zeus across nine straight nights and so became the mother of the nine Muses.

citations.title: Mnemosyne
citations.snippet: In Greek mythology, Mnemosyne was one of the Titans, who are the children of Uranus (Sky) and Gaia (Earth). She was the goddess of memory and the mother of the nine Muses by Zeus. The name Mnemosyne is derived from the Greek word mnēmē.

questions.clue: Zeus took the form of a swan to couple with Leda of Sparta, and the daughter born of that union grew into the woman whose elopement with a foreign prince launched a decade-long war.

citations.title: Helen of Troy
citations.snippet: Helen, also known as Helen of Troy, or Helen of Sparta, and in Latin as Helena, was a figure in Greek mythology said to have been the most beautiful woman in the world. She was believed to have been the daughter of Zeus by Leda or Nemesis,
